title: Difficulties in Diagnosis and Management of Adrenal Insufficiency in the Critically Ill
authors: David J. Torpy, MBBS, PhD, FRACP

items.title: Why serum cortisol may not reflect tissue glucocorticoid sufficiency in critical illness
items.text: Tissue resistance to cortisol and reduced CBG/albumin alter total versus free cortisol; serum measurements may not reflect end-organ glucocorticoid status during sepsis and other critical illnesses.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Routine measurements of serum cortisol may not reflect end-organ glucocorticoid sufficiency due to tissue resistance to cortisol and reduced concentrations of the principal carrier proteins for cortisol, corticosteroid-binding globulin (CBG) and albumin."

items.title: How critical illness-related corticosteroid insufficiency is operationally defined
items.text: CIRCI is defined as a serum cortisol increment <9 μg/dL (<248 nmol/L) 60 minutes after 250 mcg cosyntropin—though this threshold remains controversial and does not measure tissue glucocorticoid sufficiency.
items.reference: Definitions: "CIRCI is operationally defined as a serum cortisol increment of less than 9 μg/dL (<248 nmol/L) 60 minutes after administration of 250 mcg cosyntropin."

items.title: Conventional adrenal insufficiency versus CIRCI
items.text: True adrenal insufficiency shows suboptimal peak cortisol after cosyntropin (e.g., <18 μg/dL at 30 minutes, assay dependent) with ACTH distinguishing primary from secondary forms. CIRCI is a critical-care concept of impaired stress response without classic deficiency.
items.reference: Definitions: "Adrenal insufficiency is defined as the inability to produce sufficient glucocorticoids and mineralocorticoids for tissue requirements."

items.title: How critical illness remodels HPA axis physiology
items.text: Sepsis activates the HPA axis via cytokines, reduces cortisol metabolism enzymes, lowers CBG, redirects steroidogenesis toward cortisol, induces tissue glucocorticoid resistance (GR pathway changes), and is worsened by opioids, benzodiazepines, and etomidate.
items.reference: Critical Illness Stress Response: "Given the complexity of these influences on the HPA axis, especially the variable degree of tissue resistance that accompanies systemic inflammation and the fall in serum binding proteins that alter the relationship between total and free cortisol, serum cortisol concentrations may not reflect tissue glucocorticoid status."

items.title: Why a cosyntropin increment below 9 μg/dL defined relative adrenal insufficiency
items.text: In Rothwell's septic shock cohort, none of 13 patients with <9 μg/dL ACTH response survived versus 13/19 survivors with higher responses—empirically defining relative adrenal insufficiency.
items.reference: Critical Illness Stress Response: "Hence, a total plasma cortisol response to cosyntropin, 250 mcg, of less than 9 μg/dL (<248 nmol/L) empirically defines relative adrenal insufficiency."

items.title: How low-dose hydrocortisone helps septic shock
items.text: Seven days of hydrocortisone approximating stress output (200–300 mg daily) shortens vasopressor support; pressor sensitivity to norepinephrine can be restored even when mortality benefit is inconsistent across trials.
items.reference: Critical Illness Stress Response: "Longer treatment (7 days) with lower-dosage hydrocortisone approximating the normal adrenocortical cortisol output in severe stress (200-300 mg daily) shortens the need for vasopressor support."

items.title: Why the Annane 2002 septic shock trial fueled hydrocortisone use
items.text: Hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone improved 28-day mortality in cosyntropin nonresponders (<9 μg/dL increment) but not responders; high etomidate use (72/99 patients) later criticized the generalizability.
items.reference: Clinical trials: "A 2002 randomized controlled trial of hydrocortisone, 50 mg every 6 hours, and fludrocortisone, 0.05 mg daily, vs placebo revealed a 28-day mortality benefit for treatment in the nonresponders to cosyntropin, 250 mcg"

items.title: How CORTICUS tempered enthusiasm for mortality benefit
items.text: CORTICUS (499 patients) showed no 28-day mortality benefit from hydrocortisone 50 mg q6h versus placebo, though shock resolved faster; the trial was under-enrolled and criticized for enrolling less-sick patients.
items.reference: Clinical trials: "The CORTICUS trial, however, did not reveal any 28-day mortality benefit from hydrocortisone, 50 mg every 6 hours in 499 patients with septic shock"

items.title: How Venkatesh and Annane 2018 trials inform current practice
items.text: Venkatesh (ADRENAL): no 28-day mortality difference but faster shock resolution and shorter ventilation. Annane (APROCCHSS): lower 90-day mortality with hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone. Both support pressor-sparing benefit.
items.reference: Clinical trials: "No difference in 28-day mortality was observed, but a 1-day reduction in time to shock resolution (3 vs 4 days) and a 1-day reduction in initial time of mechanical ventilation (5 vs 7 days) was noted"

items.title: Why checkpoint inhibitor hypophysitis requires immediate glucocorticoids
items.text: Ipilimumab (CTLA-4 inhibitor) causes hypophysitis in up to 20% of patients. Baseline cortisol and clinical features can be diagnostic; HPA recovery is rare and high-dose steroids do not restore axis function.
items.reference: Case 1: "Ipilimumab (CTLA-4 inhibitor) produces hypophysitis in up to 20% of patients. HPA axis recovery is rare, and there is no recovery benefit from high-dosage glucocorticoid."

items.title: How hydrocortisone fits hospitalized community-acquired pneumonia
items.text: Hydrocortisone <400 mg daily for 5–7 days is suggested for hospitalized CAP based on 13 trials (12 showing mortality reduction), with hyperglycemia as the principal adverse effect—independent of cosyntropin results during acute illness.
items.reference: Case 4: "Hydrocortisone at a dosage less than 400 mg daily or the equivalent for 5 to 7 days is suggested for community-acquired pneumonia based on results from 13 trials, 12 of which showed reduced mortality."

items.title: Why free cortisol may better reflect illness severity than total cortisol
items.text: In sepsis, TNF-α and IL-6 reduce hepatic CBG synthesis and neutrophil elastase cleaves CBG at inflammatory sites; free cortisol increments correlate more closely with illness severity than total cortisol.
items.reference: Potential for Plasma Free Cortisol and CBG Measurements: "When comparing control patients with patients who have sepsis and septic shock, free cortisol increments correspond to illness severity more closely than total cortisol."

items.question: A 58-year-old man on ipilimumab for metastatic melanoma presents with hypotension, postural symptoms, fatigue, and serum cortisol 4 μg/dL. Which statement is true?
options: Cosyntropin testing must be completed before any glucocorticoid therapy
Checkpoint inhibitor-related adrenal insufficiency is likely; give parenteral glucocorticoids without delay
Most patients recover HPA axis function with high-dose oral steroids
Current exogenous glucocorticoid therapy explains the presentation
items.explanation: Clinical and biochemical features are sufficient for treatment; CTLA-4 inhibitor hypophysitis rarely recovers and high-dose steroids do not restore the axis.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: D) Checkpoint inhibitor-related adrenal insufficiency is the likely cause of the presentation, and parenteral glucocorticoids should be administered without delay"

items.question: A 75-year-old man has septic shock refractory to fluids and norepinephrine 2.0 mcg/kg/min. Random cortisol 18 μg/dL; cosyntropin rise from 14 to 17 μg/dL at 60 minutes. Best statement?
options: Hydrocortisone is contraindicated because mortality benefit is unproven
Cosyntropin nonresponse must be documented before hydrocortisone
CBG measurement is required to guide therapy
Low-dose IV hydrocortisone (~200 mg daily for 7 days) is warranted on clinical grounds
items.explanation: Septic shock unresponsive to resuscitation and vasopressors warrants hydrocortisone regardless of cosyntropin increment; pressor-sparing benefit outweighs risks of a 7-day course.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: E) The clinical circumstances warrant the use of low-dosage hydrocortisone such as intravenous 200 mg daily for 7 days without taper"

items.question: Six months after head trauma treated with dexamethasone, a 24-year-old man presents with lobar pneumonia, hypotension, cortisol 16 μg/dL, and low TSH/free T3. Best approach?
options: Serum cortisol is adequate — no glucocorticoid indicated
Start levothyroxine immediately for low TSH/T3
Prior dexamethasone explains current cortisol — withhold steroids
Hydrocortisone may be indicated for hospitalized CAP; reassess HPA axis after recovery
items.explanation: CAP requiring hospitalization is an indication for short-course low-dose hydrocortisone; thyroid and gonadal abnormalities may reflect acute illness and remote dexamethasone is not the driver.
items.reference: Case 4: "Answer: E) Hydrocortisone administration may be indicated for the community-acquired pneumonia on clinical grounds alone"

items.question: A cosyntropin stimulation test in septic shock shows a 60-minute cortisol rise of 7 μg/dL from baseline. How is this classified operationally?
options: Normal adrenal reserve
Primary adrenal insufficiency
Critical illness-related corticosteroid insufficiency (relative adrenal insufficiency)
Pituitary Cushing syndrome
items.explanation: An increment <9 μg/dL at 60 minutes after 250 mcg cosyntropin operationally defines CIRCI/relative adrenal insufficiency.
items.reference: Definitions: "CIRCI is operationally defined as a serum cortisol increment of less than 9 μg/dL (<248 nmol/L) 60 minutes after administration of 250 mcg cosyntropin."

items.question: In the 2002 Annane septic shock trial, 28-day mortality benefit with hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone was seen in which group?
options: All patients regardless of cosyntropin response
Cosyntropin responders only
Cosyntropin nonresponders (<9 μg/dL increment) only
Only patients not exposed to etomidate
items.explanation: Mortality benefit was confined to nonresponders; responders showed no mortality advantage.
items.reference: Clinical trials: "revealed a 28-day mortality benefit for treatment in the nonresponders to cosyntropin, 250 mcg (cortisol response at 30 and 60 minutes less than 9 μg/dL [<248 nmol/L]"

items.question: The CORTICUS trial of hydrocortisone in septic shock found:
options: Significant 28-day mortality reduction in all patients
No 28-day mortality benefit but more rapid shock resolution
Increased 90-day survival with fludrocortisone alone
Mandatory cosyntropin testing before enrollment improved outcomes
items.explanation: CORTICUS showed faster shock resolution without 28-day mortality improvement; it was underpowered and enrolled less-sick patients than planned.
items.reference: Clinical trials: "did not reveal any 28-day mortality benefit from hydrocortisone, 50 mg every 6 hours in 499 patients with septic shock"

items.question: The Venkatesh ADRENAL trial (2018) of continuous hydrocortisone 200 mg/day for 7 days in ventilated septic shock found:
options: Lower 28-day mortality versus placebo
No 28-day mortality difference but shorter shock duration and ventilation time
Increased bacteremia and fungemia
Benefit only in cosyntropin nonresponders
items.explanation: No mortality difference at 28 days, but 1-day reductions in shock resolution and initial mechanical ventilation without increased infection rates.
items.reference: Clinical trials: "No difference in 28-day mortality was observed, but a 1-day reduction in time to shock resolution (3 vs 4 days) and a 1-day reduction in initial time of mechanical ventilation (5 vs 7 days) was noted, with no increase in rates of bacteremia or fungemia."

items.question: The Annane 2018 APROCCHSS trial differed from ADRENAL primarily in that it showed:
options: Lower 90-day mortality with hydrocortisone plus fludrocortisone
Higher infection rates with combination therapy
No pressor-sparing effect
Benefit only with cosyntropin screening
items.explanation: APROCCHSS used hydrocortisone 50 mg q6h plus fludrocortisone and demonstrated lower 90-day (not 28-day) mortality with fewer vasopressor days.
items.reference: Clinical trials: "achieved a lower 90-day (but not 28-day) mortality in the treatment group (43% vs 49.1%), as well as fewer days requiring vasopressor support."

items.question: Current SCCM/ESICM-derived recommendations for corticosteroids in sepsis state:
options: Recommend corticosteroids for all adults with sepsis
Suggest against corticosteroids for adults with sepsis without shock
Mandate fludrocortisone in all septic patients
Require cosyntropin testing before any steroid use
items.explanation: Guidelines suggest against corticosteroids in sepsis without shock; septic shock refractory to fluids and vasopressors is the key treatment scenario.
items.reference: Guidelines: "Suggest against use of corticosteroids for adults with sepsis without shock"

items.question: According to the chapter's guideline summary, corticosteroids are recommended for:
options: Major trauma (strong recommendation)
Early moderate-to-severe ARDS and bacterial meningitis
All patients with sepsis regardless of shock
Routine post-cardiac arrest care (strong recommendation)
items.explanation: ARDS (conditional, moderate evidence) and bacterial meningitis (strong, low-quality evidence) are among recommended indications; major trauma is suggested against.
items.reference: Guidelines: "Recommendations were made for the use of corticosteroids in hospitalized patients with early moderate to severe acute respiratory distress syndrome"

items.question: Why was the 2002 Annane trial criticized regarding cosyntropin nonresponse rates?
options: Cosyntropin dose was subtherapeutic
Most patients received etomidate, which inhibits cortisol synthesis
Fludrocortisone was omitted in all patients
Patients were not ventilated
items.explanation: 72 of 99 patients received etomidate (11-hydroxylase inhibitor), potentially inflating nonresponder rates and mortality benefit in that subgroup.
items.reference: Clinical trials: "This study was criticized, as 72 of 99 patients received etomidate, an adrenal 11-hydroxylase inhibitor that reduces cortisol synthesis"

items.question: In septic shock, basal (random) cortisol is a useful mortality predictor when:
options: It is mildly elevated (20–25 μg/dL)
It is extremely high (>45 μg/dL)
It is below 5 μg/dL only
It is always predictive regardless of level
items.explanation: Basal cortisol is not generally predictive except at extremely high levels (>45 μg/dL), which portend worse outcomes.
items.reference: Critical Illness Stress Response: "Basal cortisol values in sepsis/septic shock are not useful predictors of mortality except at extremely high cortisol levels (>45 μg/dL [>1242 nmol/L])."

items.question: In Annane's 3-level prognostic classification, which septic shock patients had the lowest survival?
options: Low basal cortisol and robust cosyntropin response
High basal cortisol (>34 μg/dL) with cosyntropin increment <9 μg/dL
Normal basal cortisol and normal cosyntropin response
Low basal cortisol and low cosyntropin response
items.explanation: The combination of elevated basal cortisol with blunted ACTH response identified the subgroup with lowest survival.
items.reference: Critical Illness Stress Response: "the lowest survival rate seen in those with a basal cortisol concentration greater than 34 μg/dL (>935 nmol/L) and a cortisol response to cosyntropin at 60 minutes less than 9 μg/dL (<248 nmol/L)."

items.statement: Serum cortisol concentrations always reflect tissue glucocorticoid status in critical illness.
items.explanation: Tissue resistance, altered binding proteins, and cytokine effects decouple serum levels from end-organ sufficiency.
items.reference: Critical Illness Stress Response: "serum cortisol concentrations may not reflect tissue glucocorticoid status."

items.statement: Cosyntropin stimulation must be performed before starting hydrocortisone in septic shock refractory to vasopressors.
items.explanation: Current practice supports hydrocortisone on clinical grounds; CIRCI operational definition is not mandatory for treatment.
items.reference: Case 2: "The CIRCI operational definition is not required for hydrocortisone treatment."

items.statement: Most patients with checkpoint inhibitor-related adrenal insufficiency recover HPA axis function with high-dose glucocorticoids.
items.explanation: HPA axis recovery is rare; high-dose glucocorticoid does not restore function.
items.reference: Case 1: "HPA axis recovery is rare, and there is no recovery benefit from high-dosage glucocorticoid."

items.statement: Hydrocortisone is suggested for hospitalized community-acquired pneumonia at doses below 400 mg daily for 5–7 days.
items.explanation: 13 trials, 12 showing mortality reduction, support conditional recommendation for hospitalized CAP.
items.reference: Case 4: "Hydrocortisone at a dosage less than 400 mg daily or the equivalent for 5 to 7 days is suggested for community-acquired pneumonia"

items.statement: The CORTICUS trial demonstrated a 28-day mortality benefit from hydrocortisone in septic shock.
items.explanation: No 28-day mortality benefit was seen, though shock resolved more quickly.
items.reference: Clinical trials: "did not reveal any 28-day mortality benefit from hydrocortisone, 50 mg every 6 hours in 499 patients with septic shock"

items.statement: Hydrocortisone has an established mortality benefit in severe trauma.
items.explanation: A place for hydrocortisone in severe trauma is not established; guidelines suggest against use in major trauma.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "A place for hydrocortisone in severe trauma is not established."

items.statement: Free cortisol increments correlate more closely with illness severity in sepsis than total cortisol.
items.explanation: Reduced CBG and cleavage at inflammatory sites increase free fraction; free cortisol tracks severity better.
items.reference: Potential for Plasma Free Cortisol and CBG Measurements: "free cortisol increments correspond to illness severity more closely than total cortisol."

items.statement: The ADRENAL trial showed increased rates of bacteremia and fungemia with hydrocortisone.
items.explanation: Venkatesh et al reported no increase in bacteremia or fungemia despite faster shock resolution.
items.reference: Clinical trials: "with no increase in rates of bacteremia or fungemia."

items.assertion: Assertion: Random serum cortisol is unreliable for assessing glucocorticoid sufficiency in sepsis.
items.reason: Reason: Tissue resistance and reduced CBG/albumin alter the relationship between total and free cortisol.
items.explanation: Both true and causally linked—binding protein changes and tissue resistance decouple serum from tissue status.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Routine measurements of serum cortisol may not reflect end-organ glucocorticoid sufficiency due to tissue resistance to cortisol and reduced concentrations of the principal carrier proteins for cortisol, corticosteroid-binding globulin (CBG) and albumin."

items.assertion: Assertion: Hydrocortisone is indicated in septic shock refractory to fluids and vasopressors.
items.reason: Reason: Low-dose hydrocortisone for 7 days shortens vasopressor requirement.
items.explanation: Both true; pressor-sparing benefit supports treatment even when mortality trials are discordant.
items.reference: Case 2: "Hydrocortisone use is recommended in septic shock unresponsive to fluid resuscitation and inotropes."

items.assertion: Assertion: Cosyntropin testing is mandatory before hydrocortisone in septic shock.
items.reason: Reason: CIRCI is defined as a cosyntropin increment less than 9 μg/dL at 60 minutes.
items.explanation: Both true but R does not justify A—definition exists but is not required for treatment decisions.
items.reference: Case 2: "The CIRCI operational definition is not required for hydrocortisone treatment."

items.assertion: Assertion: A patient on ipilimumab with cortisol 4 μg/dL and shock should receive immediate parenteral glucocorticoids.
items.reason: Reason: Cosyntropin stimulation is needed to distinguish adrenal insufficiency from CIRCI.
items.explanation: A is true; R is false—clinical presentation is sufficient without delaying for cosyntropin.
items.reference: Case 1: "Baseline cortisol and clinical features are sufficiently diagnostic of adrenal insufficiency."

items.assertion: Assertion: The Annane 2002 trial proved mortality benefit of hydrocortisone in all septic shock patients.
items.reason: Reason: Benefit was limited to cosyntropin nonresponders.
items.explanation: A is false (benefit only in nonresponders); R is true.
items.reference: Clinical trials: "revealed a 28-day mortality benefit for treatment in the nonresponders to cosyntropin, 250 mcg"

items.assertion: Assertion: CORTICUS showed no mortality benefit from hydrocortisone in septic shock.
items.reason: Reason: Hydrocortisone accelerated shock resolution in that trial.
items.explanation: Both true; lack of mortality benefit coexisted with faster shock resolution.
items.reference: Clinical trials: "Hydrocortisone, however, was associated with more rapid resolution of shock."

items.assertion: Assertion: Hydrocortisone may be given for hospitalized community-acquired pneumonia without proven primary adrenal insufficiency.
items.reason: Reason: Multiple randomized trials show mortality reduction with short-course low-dose steroids.
items.explanation: Both true; CAP indication is evidence-based and independent of formal AI diagnosis.
items.reference: Case 4: "Hydrocortisone at a dosage less than 400 mg daily or the equivalent for 5 to 7 days is suggested for community-acquired pneumonia based on results from 13 trials, 12 of which showed reduced mortality."

items.assertion: Assertion: Hydrocortisone should be routinely used in major trauma.
items.reason: Reason: Severe trauma causes profound HPA axis activation requiring supplementation.
items.explanation: Both false—trauma HPA activation does not establish benefit; guidelines suggest against routine use.
items.reference: Guidelines: "Suggest against use of corticosteroids in major trauma (low-quality of evidence)"
